title: Developing a national identity
description: Check your understanding of Colonial North America, from the Puritans to the Great Awakening.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What role did religion play in the Massachusetts Bay Colony in the 1600s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about how Puritan leaders responded when people like Anne Hutchinson or Roger Williams disagreed with them.

choices.content: The colony enforced strict rules based on the Puritan faith.
The colony allowed people of all faiths to worship freely.
The colony welcomed all Christian sects but no other religions.
The colony separated church and state to avoid religious conflict.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Puritans and Separatists differ in their attitude toward the Church of England?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Ask yourself which group wanted to *change* the Church of England and which group wanted to *separate* from it entirely.

choices.content: Puritans wanted to reform the Church from within, while Separatists chose to break from the Church completely.
Puritans supported most Church practices but wanted to improve leadership, while Separatists rejected the Church completely.
Puritans sought to preserve traditional Church rituals and practice, while Separatists demanded stricter moral standards.
Puritans valued religious tolerance, while Separatists thought the Church of England was the only true religious authority.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why was the Mayflower Compact of 1620 an important development in colonial America?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Focus on how the Pilgrims agreed to organize their community before even stepping off the ship.

choices.content: It established a form of self-government based on majority rule.
It created an official state church for the New England colonies.
It marked the first colonial declaration of independence from England.
It guaranteed equal political rights to all men and women in the colony.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Massachusetts Bay governor John Winthrop describe his vision for the colony?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Winthrop said he wanted Massachusetts Bay to serve as a model for others.

choices.content: As a "city upon a hill," setting an example of Puritan faith for the world
As a place for "sinners in the hands of an angry God," those who had left the Church of England
As a "closed frontier" meant to block Native Americans and rival Europeans
As a "great experiment" in religious tolerance, welcoming all settlers

assessment_items.question_content:
**What do the experiences of Roger Williams and Anne Hutchinson show about leadership in the Massachusetts Bay Colony?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about what happened when Williams and Hutchinson disagreed with Puritan leaders—were they welcomed, or pushed out?

choices.content: Leaders expected everyone to follow rules and punished those who disagreed.
Leaders encouraged open debate about religion as part of community life.
Leaders protected people who challenged church teachings from unfair treatment.
Leaders promoted tolerance by welcoming a variety of Christian groups into the colony.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the Great Awakening of the 1700s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think of passionate sermons and revival meetings—this was about stirring faith, not science or politics.

choices.content: A wave of religious revivals that encouraged conversion and renewed faith
A movement that celebrated science, logic, and rational thought
A religious movement against Puritan rule after Oliver Cromwell came to power
A campaign to expand colonial self-government and representative assemblies

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the Enlightenment in the 1700s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Enlightenment is often called the "age of reason."

choices.content: An intellectual movement that emphasized reason, science, and natural rights
A religious revival that called people back to strict faith through emotional preaching
A cultural movement that focused on celebrating classical art, architecture, and literature
A political movement to strengthen the power of monarchies across Europe

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did England’s policy of salutary (or benign) neglect shape colonial politics before the American Revolution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about how neglect gave colonists space to build habits of self-government.

choices.content: It allowed colonies to develop local assemblies and traditions of self-rule.
It ended colonial governments and replaced them with direct royal rule.
It convinced colonists that Parliament cared deeply about their everyday concerns.
It unified all thirteen colonies under a single centralized government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did the Navigation Acts cause tension between Britain and the colonies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about why colonial merchants often chose to smuggle goods instead of following trade laws.

choices.content: They allowed the colonies to export goods only to Britain.
They forced colonists to buy manufactured goods from Britain.
They raised customs duties on goods imported into the colonies from Britain.
They required colonial ships to serve in Britain’s navy.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What do King Philip’s War in New England and the Anglo-Powhatan Wars in Virginia reveal about English colonization?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Think about what both wars showed about how English colonists expanded their control of land and dealt with Native resistance.

choices.content: English colonists expanded settlement through violent conflicts that greatly weakened Native communities.
English colonists depended heavily on Native Americans for survival and protection throughout the colonial period.
Native Americans successfully maintained control over colonial settlement policies until the early 1700s.
English colonists learned that maintaining peace with Native nations was the best way to expand their settlements.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which choice correctly describes how triangular trade connected Europe, Africa, and the Americas?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Focus on the major goods that moved across the Atlantic in large numbers, especially the labor and crops that drove the system.

choices.content: Crops to Europe, manufactured goods to Africa, and enslaved people to the Americas
Sugar and tea to the Americas, timber to Africa, and iron and manufactured goods to Europe
Crops to Africa, manufactured goods to Europe, and tea and enslaved people to the Americas
Gold to the Americas, furs to Europe, and spices and manufactured goods to Africa

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was one major effect of the consumer revolution in the late 1600s and early 1700s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Focus on how the consumer revolution changed daily life and culture, not on government or independence.

choices.content: It tied Britain and the colonies together through shared goods, books, and fashions.
It encouraged colonists to wear homespun clothes as a protest against British taxes on goods.
It turned the southern colonies into a hub of manufacturing and British banking.
It caused colonists to push for self-government and separation from Britain.
